titleStmt.title: Despatch from Owen L. W. Smith to David J. Hill, November 23, 1900

distributor.name: Center for Digital Research in the Humanities

availability.p: Licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 Unported License. Derivatives must be credited to To Enter Africa from America, made available non-commercially, and distributed under the same terms. Requests for permission for commercial publication or other use should be emailed to the project team.

bibl.title: Despatch from Owen L. W. Smith to David J. Hill, November 23, 1900
Despatches from United States Consuls in Monrovia, 1852-1906

correspAction.roleName: U.S. Minister Resident and Consul General

div1.p:
No. 54, Consular,
Legation of the United States,
Monrovia Liberia;
Nov. 23, 1900.

Hon. David J. Hill,
Assistant Secretary of State,
Washington.
Evidence of the solvency of the sureties on the official bond of Vice Consul General, Beverly Y. Payne.
Vice Consul General, Beverly Y. Payne, died in the United States, sometime during the month of Sept. 1900.

No. 54, Consular,
Legation of the United States,
Monrovia Liberia;
Nov. 23, 1900.

Hon. David J. Hill,
Assistant Secretary of State,
Washington.

I have the honor to acknowledge the receipt, Nov. 22, 1900, of your note of Oct. 18, 1900, through the Hon. Thos. W. Cridler, third Assistant Secretary of State, to Beverly Y. Payne Esq., deceased, late United States Vice Consul General to Liberia, requesting him to furnish the Department with renewed assurances of the solvency of his sureties on his official bond, approved Nov. 9, 1898.

In reply, I have to call your
your attention to my No. 50 "Consular," of Oct. 26, 1900, reporting the death of Vice Consul General Beverly Y. Payne, in the United States during the month of September 1900.

In view of the death of Vice Consul General, Beverly Y. Payne; and inasmuch as he had not discharged any official services, during his recent term of office, thus leaving no official accounts awaiting settlement &c by the State Department, I have not thought it necessary to request his sureties to furnish renewed evidence of their solvency &c.
